title: The Testing Pyramid
intro: Not all tests are created equal. Some are fast and cheap, others are slow and expensive. Just like a building needs a wide, solid foundation and only a small penthouse on top, the testing pyramid helps you decide how many of each type to write. A team has their pyramid upside-down -- find the problem.

teach.title: The Pyramid = A Building's Foundation
teach.body: A building needs a wide, solid foundation (cheap, fast), regular floors in the middle, and a small penthouse on top (expensive, fancy). If you build a building that's mostly penthouse with a tiny foundation, it topples over. The **testing pyramid** works the same way -- you need many fast, cheap tests at the bottom and fewer slow, expensive tests at the top.

teach.title: The Pyramid = A Healthcare System
teach.body: A good healthcare system has many quick checkups (blood pressure, temperature) that catch most problems early, some specialized tests (blood work, X-rays) for deeper issues, and few expensive procedures (MRIs, surgery) for serious cases. The **testing pyramid** follows the same logic -- lots of quick unit tests catch most bugs, integration tests catch connection issues, and a few expensive E2E tests verify critical flows.

teach.title: The Three Layers
teach.body:
The testing pyramid has three layers:

1. **Unit tests** (bottom, MANY) -- Test individual functions. Super fast, run in milliseconds. Write lots of these.
2. **Integration tests** (middle, SOME) -- Test how pieces work together. Slower, but catch connection bugs.
3. **End-to-end (E2E) tests** (top, FEW) -- Test the whole app like a real user. Slowest, but test the full flow.

The ratio should be roughly: 70% unit, 20% integration, 10% E2E.

teach.title: Speed Comparison
teach.code:
# Unit tests: test a single function
# Speed: ~50 tests per second
npm run test:unit     # Done in 2 seconds

# Integration tests: test API endpoints
# Speed: ~5 tests per second
npm run test:int      # Done in 30 seconds

# E2E tests: test full user flows in a browser
# Speed: ~1 test per 10 seconds
npm run test:e2e      # Done in 5 minutes
teach.annotation: Unit tests run 100x faster than E2E tests. That's why you want more of them.

teach.body: A slow test suite means slow deploys, and slow deploys mean you can't react to users fast enough. The pyramid keeps your tests blazing fast so you can ship AI-built features daily -- the speed that turns a side project into a business.
The testing pyramid says: write many fast unit tests, some integration tests, and few slow E2E tests. This gives you the best balance of speed and coverage.

exercise.prompt: This team's test suite takes 45 minutes to run and their CI pipeline is painfully slow. Looking at their test distribution, which layer is the problem?

fixes.text: # E2E tests:         10 tests (move logic to unit tests)

exercise.explanation: The team has an inverted testing pyramid -- 85 E2E tests and only 5 unit tests. E2E tests are the slowest type (each one opens a browser and simulates clicks), so 85 of them take forever. The fix is to move most of that testing logic into fast unit tests and keep only the critical user flows as E2E tests. This follows the pyramid: many unit tests at the base, few E2E tests at the top.
